top_categories.workout_context: 59ft/mi means long efforts at or near FTP on every climb. If your threshold cracks at minute 15, you'll walk the rest.
At mile 60 of 100, glycogen is gone. This workout teaches your body to produce watts on fumes.
At Clare Classic, surges push you above threshold and you have to recover while still riding. Over-unders train exactly that.
88–92% FTP is where you'll spend the hardest sustained miles of Clare Classic. Train it until it feels boring.
Race-pace efforts that mimic Clare Classic's demands. Pacing, fueling, and tactical decisions under fatigue. Practice the race before February.
5,906ft of climbing on grades that change every minute. You need to switch from seated to standing without losing rhythm.
Clare Classic's mixed terrain forces constant power changes. Surge over the rough stuff, settle on the smooth, repeat for 100 miles.
5,906ft of climbing means repeated surges above threshold. VO2max work is how you survive the fifth climb, not just the first.
Two threshold sessions per week without the recovery cost of VO2max work. For 100 miles, sustainable FTP gains matter more than peak power.
100 miles rewards fat oxidation. This volume work shifts your fuel mix so you're still burning fat at hour 5, not bonking at hour 3.
Every hard session works better with a bigger aerobic base. At 100 miles, base fitness is the difference between racing and surviving.
Endurance with intensity spikes baked in. Exactly what 5,906ft of climbing on mixed terrain does to your power file on race day.

race_overlay.nutrition: Target 60–80g carbs/hour for Clare Classic’s 100 miles. Start fueling within the first 30 minutes — early fueling prevents late-race collapse. Bonking at mile 60 is a nutrition failure, not a fitness failure.

pack_summary: This 10-workout pack focuses on TT Threshold, Durability, and Over Under to prepare you for 100 miles of mixed terrain in Clare Valley, South Australia.
